summary: In this advice column installment, Aunt Pythia (Cathy O'Neil) fields questions on amphetamine use in academia, school safety statistics, introversion in corporate settings, lottery prediction systems, and diversity in data science. She draws on Paul Erdos's well-known benzedrine use to argue that drug use isn't a moral issue unless it affects responsibilities to others, and theorizes that stimulant users in math sacrifice personal life for productivity. She gives characteristically blunt advice across topics, dismissing lottery prediction schemes and encouraging the questioner on diversity to consider how homogeneity limits companies.
key_points: Stimulant use in academia is widespread and not inherently a moral issue — it only becomes one when it affects responsibilities to others
Drugs likely don't make people smarter, they just enable more efficient focus, and users often sacrifice personal life for single-minded productivity
Teachers should worry more about standardized testing and professional vilification than about armed violence
Lack of diversity in data science introduces selection bias into how companies understand their users, shrinking their potential
Lottery prediction systems based on historical patterns are futile — the best system is not playing at all
money_quotes: As far as I'm concerned drug use isn't a moral issue at all, unless it leaks out into people's responsibilities to others.
I don't actually think drugs make you smarter, they just let you focus more efficiently.

Be good at something, so you can be successful, so you can get laid. Someone who is forgoing sex for the sake of being good at something is therefore, in my framework, sacrificing the end for the means.
As far as the case at hand, namely teachers, I'd worry more about standardized testing and the vilification of my profession than about armed killers.
Yes, yes I do have a better system. I call my system 'don't play the lottery.'
I think there's an enormous amount of selection bias in the ways companies think about their users and what they want, and they shrink their potential by having only narrow views.
key_insight: Whether discussing stimulant use, school safety, or workforce diversity, O'Neil consistently cuts through moral panic and conventional framing to focus on structural incentives and statistical thinking.
